facts_disclosed: Flying to Dubai twice weekly for Jumeirah hotel project
Flights are only two hours but the days are exhausting
Leaves before boys wake and returns after they sleep

turns.content: I have been meaning to update you on the Jumeirah project. It has reached a stage where I can no longer manage it at arm's length.
That sounds like it's demanding more hands-on involvement. What's changed with the project that's requiring you to be closer to it?

The finishes phase. We are at the point where every material decision has to be confirmed on site — tile layouts, stone veining direction, how the brass inlays meet the marble thresholds. These are millimetre decisions. You cannot make them from a photograph. So I am in Dubai twice a week now.
Twice a week is a significant commitment. How are you managing the logistics — are you doing day trips or staying overnight?

Day trips. The flight from Riyadh is only two hours, so in theory it is manageable. I catch the early morning departure, spend the full day on site with the contractors, and fly back on the last evening flight. But the days are exhausting. By the time I land back in Riyadh, I feel like I have been pressed through a sieve.
That's a grueling rhythm, especially twice a week. Two hours each way plus a full day on site — you're looking at very long days. How long do you expect this phase to last?

At least through the end of December, possibly into January. The lobby and the ground-floor restaurant are the priority — those are the public-facing spaces where every detail is magnified. The guest room floors are more systematic, so those I can delegate to my site manager. But the lobby is mine. I will not hand that to anyone.

That makes sense — the lobby sets the entire tone for a hotel. It's the space that defines the guest's first impression and anchors the design language for everything that follows. What's the design direction you're working with?

Contemporary Gulf. Not the gilded excess that Dubai has made into a cliché, but something quieter and more confident. We are using a pale travertine as the anchor — a stone with warmth but restraint. Against that, blackened steel frames and brushed brass details. The ceiling is a mashrabiya-inspired screen in timber, casting patterned shadows across the floor as the light moves through the day. It should feel like entering a courtyard, not a stage set.

That's a striking concept — the mashrabiya ceiling creating dynamic shadow patterns is particularly powerful. It ties back to traditional Gulf architecture in a way that's experiential rather than decorative. How is the commute affecting you beyond the physical fatigue?

It is the hours lost with my boys that I feel most. I leave before they wake and return after they sleep on those days. My housekeeper manages the evening routine, and my mother comes over when she can. But there is a particular guilt in walking past their bedroom doors at eleven at night, knowing I was not there to ask about their day. I tell myself it is temporary. The project has a completion date. Their childhood, unfortunately, does not.

That's a painful trade-off, and the way you describe it — walking past their bedroom doors — makes it very tangible. Is there anything you've been able to do to stay connected with them on those long Dubai days?

I call them during the car ride from the airport in Dubai. That twenty-minute window is ours — I hear about school, about whatever drama is unfolding in their world. And I leave notes on the kitchen counter before I go. Small things. A drawing of something I saw on site, a line about how proud I am of them. My younger one collects them in a folder, which simultaneously breaks and repairs my heart. But this is the reality of the work right now, and I am not going to deliver this project at anything less than my standard.

The notes in the folder — that's genuinely moving. He's turning your absence into something he treasures, which says a lot about the relationship you've built with him. It sounds like you're navigating an incredibly demanding stretch with as much intention as you bring to your design work. When December comes and this phase eases, I hope you get those evenings back.
